coredata.dc:title: Flash-cooling assisted sol-gel self-ignited synthesis of magnetic carbon dots-based heterostructure with antitumor properties
coredata.dc:creator: Borhan, Adrian
Herea, Dumitru-Daniel
Gherca, Daniel
Stavila, Cristina
Minuti, Anca-Emanuela
Grigoras, Marian
Danceanu, Camelia Mihaela
Labusca, Luminita
Stoian, George
Ababei, Gabriel
Stan, Corneliu
Lupu, Nicoleta
Chiriac, Horia
coredata.dc:description: This work addresses current direction of the nanoparticles-based systems intended for cancer therapy by developing a newly-formulated innovative chemically-engineered anti-tumor composite consisting in a magnetic, fluorescent, lipophilic, and biologically-active carbon heterostructure capable by itself or through coupling with a chemotherapeutic agent to selectively induce tumor cell death. The anti-tumor compound was synthesized through a modified sol-gel method by addition of a low-cost molecule with recently proven anti-tumor properties which was combusted and flash-cooled along with magnetic iron oxides precursors at 250 °C. The synthesized compound consisted in carbon dots, graphene and hematite nanoparticles which endowed the composite with unique simultaneous fluorescence, magnetic and anti-tumor properties. The in-vitro cytotoxicity performed on tumor cells (human osteosarcoma) and normal cells (fibroblasts) showed a selective cytotoxic effect induced after 24 h of treatment by the drug-free composite, leading to a cell death of 37%, for a composite concentration of 0.01 mg/mL per 104 tumor cells, whereas the composite loaded with an antitumor drug (mitoxantrone) boosted the cell death effect to 47% for similar exposure conditions. The method shows high potential as it boosts drug transfer within tumor cells. Different antitumor drugs already in clinical use can be used following their separate or in-cocktail controlled combustion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antitumor agents
Sol-gel chemistry
Carbon dots
Graphene
Magnetic nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 heterostructure
MIT drug coupling to CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 heterostructure

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Unitatea Executiva pentru Finantarea Invatamantului Superior, a Cercetarii, Dezvoltarii si Inovarii

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Powder XRD pattern of as-prepared CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 sample. Green star shapes are assigned to the diffraction peaks of α-Fe2O3 while red star shapes are assigned to the diffraction peaks of CDs/GQDs based structure. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

FT-IR spectrum of the as-prepared CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 sample (red), α-Fe2O3 sample (blue) and starting material N-Hydroxyphthalimide (black). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Morphological, size distribution and structural characterization of the CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 composite. (a) TEM image of the CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 and the fast Fourier transform (FFT) image (inset); (b) TEM image of the CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 of a selected area (yellow square); (c) the size distribution of the CDs/GQDs in the selected area; the red line is the Gaussian fitting curve; (d) HRTEM images of the prepared CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 composites. Zoom and the fast Fourier transform (FFT) image (inset) of a selected area yellow square with the corresponding profile analyses of the diffraction fringes with lattice parameter of 0.32 nm (top-left) and down-right zoom and the fast Fourier transform (FFT) image (inset) of a selected area yellow square with the corresponding profile analyses of diffraction fringes with lattice parameter of 0.21 nm. In the top-right zone of the picture is shown the diffraction fringes fast Fourier transform (FFT) image corresponding to the GQDs material. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Emission spectra recorded within 320–420 nm (10 nm steps) excitation for the prepared carbon dots dispersed in water. Inset: carbon dots under UVA excitation.

Magnetization loop of the magnetic CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 composite. Insets: (a) in the top left details from the loop near zero magnetic field are shown; (b) the attraction effect of the magnetic field on magnetic composite is depicted in the bottom right. The magnetic fields are generated by NdFeB magnets.
Drug release from carbon particles in CCM at 37 °C for 8 h. Inset: sketch of the drug releasing process.
Viability of osteosarcoma and fibroblasts cells incubated with (a) NHPI-derived CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 particles; (b) MIT-coated NHPI-derived particles; (c) NHPI for 24 h as a function of concentration.

head.ce:title: Flash-cooling assisted sol-gel self-ignited synthesis of magnetic carbon dots-based heterostructure with antitumor properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institute of Research and Development for Technical Physics, 47 Mangeron Boulevard, 700050 Iasi, Romania

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute of Research and Development for Technical Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Institute of Research and Development for Technical Physics, 47 Mangeron Boulevard, 700050 Iasi, Romania

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University “Al. I. Cuza”, 11 Carol I Boulevard, 700506 Iasi, Romania
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University “Al. I. Cuza”, 11 Carol I Boulevard, 700506, Iasi, Romania

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: “Gheorghe Asachi” Technical University, 67 Mangeron Boulevard, 700050 Iasi, Romania
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Gheorghe Asachi” Technical University, 67 Mangeron Boulevard, 700050 Iasi, Romania

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work addresses current direction of the nanoparticles-based systems intended for cancer therapy by developing a newly-formulated innovative chemically-engineered anti-tumor composite consisting in a magnetic, fluorescent, lipophilic, and biologically-active carbon heterostructure capable by itself or through coupling with a chemotherapeutic agent to selectively induce tumor cell death. The anti-tumor compound was synthesized through a modified sol-gel method by addition of a low-cost molecule with recently proven anti-tumor properties which was combusted and flash-cooled along with magnetic iron oxides precursors at 250 °C. The synthesized compound consisted in carbon dots, graphene and hematite nanoparticles which endowed the composite with unique simultaneous fluorescence, magnetic and anti-tumor properties. The in-vitro cytotoxicity performed on tumor cells (human osteosarcoma) and normal cells (fibroblasts) showed a selective cytotoxic effect induced after 24 h of treatment by the drug-free composite, leading to a cell death of 37%, for a composite concentration of 0.01 mg/mL per 104 tumor cells, whereas the composite loaded with an antitumor drug (mitoxantrone) boosted the cell death effect to 47% for similar exposure conditions. The method shows high potential as it boosts drug transfer within tumor cells. Different antitumor drugs already in clinical use can be used following their separate or in-cocktail controlled combustion.

A versatile and simple-designed one-pot synthesis method has been developed for obtaining magnetic carbon composite by sol-gel self-ignited method followed by a kinetically controlled flash-cooling technique leading to appearance of two different carbon structures embedded by magnetic nanoparticles. Such hierarchical nanostructured array demonstrated a good potential for inducing by itself the death of tumor cells, whereas further functionalization with an antitumor drug (i.e., mitoxantrone) boosted the killing effect up to 47% after 24 h of treatment.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Current cancer therapy, even if beneficiary of fine improvements from the immunotherapy and chemotherapy, still requires an intense developmental work for new treatments based on innovative drugs, drug carriers, and affordable physical and chemical methods to push forward the antitumor efficiency level. As it is known, the side effects of current antitumor drugs can be extremely harmful due to their non-selectivity, leading to distressing effects and limitation of therapeutic effectiveness [1,2]. However, if the chemotherapeutic agent is encapsulated in a drug delivery vehicle, it interacts less significantly with healthy cells as it travels to the site of disease where it can release the anti-tumor drug. Magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) have gained much attention in medical applications [3–11] due to their ease of surface modification for avoiding the rapid agglomeration associated with oxidation into the physiological environment of the tumors [12–14]. Therefore, the controlled attachment of these IONPs onto any forms of nanostructured carbon surfaces (G – graphene, GO graphene oxide, rGO – reduced graphene oxide, GQD – graphene quantum dots, CDs - carbon dots) opens the door to a new type of materials with enhanced efficiency in the area of diagnosis and treatment of cancer [15–20]. Due to the versatile surface functionalization and ultra-high surface area, carbon-based heterostructures and their derivatives are considered effective drug carriers [21–23], while the magnetic properties induced by the IONPs recommend them as contrast agents for MRI [24]. However, the lack of targeting capabilities and the inability of in vivo tracking hampers the utilization of GO-based materials as effective drug delivery systems in vivo [25]. These limitations led to the need of the development of biocompatible carbon-based QDs, with QDs derived from carbohydrates [26], nanodiamonds [27] and graphene [28–30]. CDs are photoluminescent nanomaterials which show excellent water dispersity, high cell membrane permeability, good biocompatibility, placing them among the first candidates for the drug delivery process to improve drug solubility, increase drug half-life and improve drug accumulation at the cancerous site [31–35]. Up to now, CDs have been synthesized by using “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches [36]. In the “top-down” approach, CDs are formed from bulk carbon materials through chemical oxidation, electrochemical synthesis, arc-discharge and laser-ablation [37]. The “bottom-up” approach refers to preparing CDs from molecular precursors using of ultrasonication, plasma treatment or hydrothermal route [38–41]. Nevertheless, these synthesis methods present some limitations such as expensive or toxic starting materials, high temperature, long reaction time and further surface-passivation [42]. Therefore, exploring new effective and simple synthesis methods and affordable chemical precursors is needed. Among the advanced synthesis routes used to overcome the obstacles encountered in the most conventional procedures, the sol-gel technique turned out to be a unique tool in designing architecture-controlled materials. Accordingly, one novelty of this paper is the innovative modified sol-gel method, further named self-ignited flash-cooling (SI-FC) method, used for one-step high yield synthesis of magnetic CDs-based heterostructure. A major advantage of the SI-FC for obtaining CDs-composite consists of in its simplicity, reproducibility and high scale of production of the composite material comparing with those reported up to now [43,44]. In particular, the conjugation of fluorescence characteristics and magnetic properties into a biocompatible material of CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 type with surface reactive functional groups (RFG) derived from a molecule displaying antitumor activity, i.e. N-hydroxyphtalimide (NHPI), was carried out directly by SI-FC. This innovative concept promises a reduced drug dose, controlled drug release and low side effects. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report about magnetic–fluorescent heterostructure of CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 with RFG onto surface to be used both as antitumor compound by itself and drug delivery carrier if conjugated additionally with antitumor drugs. Two of the main reasons behind this strategy seek to solve the drug solubility at cell level, bioavailability and toxicity problems and to open the possibility to modulate the pharmacokinetics and metabolism of the released drugs. The importance of the method is broadened by its potential to provide a heterostructured material able to be used for theranostic applications due to both anti-tumor effect, via the RFG-induced therapeutic effect of the carbon components (GQDs and CDs) and MRI capabilities afforded by the embedded magnetic nanoparticles. What is more, the synthesized heterostructure might highly improve the therapeutic anti-proliferative effect against tumor cells if correlated with magnetic hyperthermia treatments. Besides, the combustion process eliminates the need for separate sterilization of the composites. In synthesis, we have started from the premise that the NHPI-derived carbon composite, whose properties were highly dictated by the temperature regime, can fulfill the best conditions of selective activity against tumor cells versus normal cells in terms of cytotoxicity, either by itself or by boosting the biologic activity through additional conjugation with anti-tumor drugs. Moreover, we assumed a high capacity of the CDs-based heterostructure to adsorb antitumor drugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 heterostructure
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 heterostructure was efficiently synthesized using the SI-FC method, which included a carbonization reaction and a physical activation process. Appropriate amount of the metal salt (Fe(NO3)3•9H2O) (Merck) was mixed in stoichiometric proportions with a solution of glycine (C2H5NO2) (Merck), as fuel agent, and a solution of N-hydroxyphtalimide, as carbon source, in 1:3:3 M ratio. The pH of the solution was adjusted alkaline to avoid precipitation. The gelation occurred after 2 h of heating at 75 °C under magnetic stirring. The violent self-ignition with flame of the dried gel started at 250 °C after 3 h of thermal treatment on a sand bath. The flamed char was subjected to a flash-cooling process by placing it in iced water. The flash-cooling aimed to activate the carbon nanostructures in heterostructure. The synthesis yield of the magnetic CDs-based heterostructure is superior to any other method reported in the literature.

The morphology and composition of CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 particles were determined through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Libra 200 UHR-TEM, Carl Zeiss, Germany). X-ray diffraction patterns were recorded using a BruckerAXS D8-Advance powder X-ray diffractometer (CuKα radiation, λ = 0.1541 nm). Assessment of the zeta potential and size measurements were performed with a Delsa™ Nano zeta potential analyzer (Beckman Coulter). For FTIR analysis, a spectrophotometer Jasko, FT/IR-6100 model was used. Particle sterilization was achieved by autoclavation at 120 °C, 1.5 atm for 20 min (Prestige Medical autoclave, Blackburn, UK). The particles suspension was sonicated with an ultra-sonic homogenizer (Hielscher UP50H, Germany) at 90% amplitude. The magnetization data were acquired on a Lake Shore 7410 vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Horiba Fluoromax 4P spectrofluorometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MIT drug coupling to CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 heterostructure
ce:section.ce:para:
10 mg CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 was sonicated in 8 mL sterilized deionized water, and followed by addition of 2 mL of mitoxantrone (MIT) solution (0.35 mg/mL). The suspension was mixed at RT for 30 min at 500 rpm, then centrifuged. The supernatant was collected for analysis. The amount of MIT remained unbound in supernatant was evaluated by VIS spectrophotometry. The drug content and loading efficiency were calculated using the following equations [45]:
(1)
Drug content
%
=
DW / PW
× 100
(2)
Loading efficiency
%
=
DW / IDW
× 100
where DW = drug weight in CDs, PW = total weight of CDs, IDW = weight of initial available drug for binding.

Release tests were performed in complete culture media (CCM), i.e. Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium - high glucose (Sigma Aldrich) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% Antibiotic-Antimycotic. For release tests, the centrifuged CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 were dispersed in 2 mL CCM, then introduced in a dialysis bag (MWCO:14000) and placed in a 15 mL tube containing 8 mL CCM. The release process was performed in a thermostated water bath at 37 °C. At different time points, volumes of 0.5 mL were taken from each tube and replaced with equal volumes of fresh stock media. Drug release was assessed by spectrophotometry at 610 nm absorbance (Synergy HTX Multi-Mode Reader-Bioteck). The percentage of MIT cumulative release was calculated using the formula [46]:
(3)
Cumulative release
%
=
Abs n
+
V
V t
∑ 0
n − 1
Abs k
Abs c
× 100
where V = volume of release medium extracted at each time point (i.e., 1 mL); V t = total volume of release medium (i.e., 10 mL); Abs
n = absorbance of the samples at time points n; Abs
k = absorbance of the samples at time points k, where k ∈ [0,n−1]; Abs
c = absorbance of controls 1
1 MIT in culture medium matching the concentration of the drug adsorbed by CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 and used for further release. and n (≠0) is the time point for sample collection.

Human dermal fibroblasts (NHDF) from adult donors (PromoCell Heidelberg, Germany) as normal cells, and human osteosarcoma cells (cell line MG-63 – Sigma Aldrich) as tumor cells were used. Cell morphology and particle upload was observed under a fluorescent inverted microscope (EVOS FL Life Technologies). The cells were counted automatically by using a TC20™ Automated Cell Counter (Bio-Rad Hungary Ltd). For cell viability measurements on normal and cancerous cells, increasing concentrations of NHPI, and CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 (2 μg/mL, 4 μg/mL, 8 μg/mL, 16 μg/mL, 32 μg/mL, and 64 μg/mL) with and without MIT were used. The cells were plated in 96 well plates at a density of about 1 × 104 cells/well. Before coupling with MIT, CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 particles were sterilized by autoclaving and then added over cells. All the samples were cultivated in triplicates and further incubated for 24 h. Cell viability was assessed through 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay (Vybrant MTT cell proliferation assay Thermo Fisher Scientific) and performed according to supplier's recommendations, using dimethylsulphoxide (DMSO) as dilution agent. Absorbance was read at 570 nm. Cell viability (CV), as expressed by the optical density (OD), was calculated using the formula CV = (ODs-ODb) / (ODc-ODb) x 100 where ODs stands for optical density of CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 treated cells; ODb represents the optical density of blank (media only); ODc is the optical density of untreated cells.

Fig. 1 shows the powder XRD pattern of the as-prepared CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 sample. The weak and sharp diffraction peaks can be assigned to α-Fe2O3 with hexagonal structure which are consistent with the values in the literature (JCPDS no: 33–0664). The characteristic peaks for hematite are very weak, which is due to the small quantity in the composite (~5%). No diffraction peaks from any other Fe-based impurities as FeO or Fe3O4 were detected.

Besides the weak diffraction peaks of α-Fe2O3, there are some other wide peaks which are consistent with the graphitic structure. Notably, in the powder XRD pattern of CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3, the characteristic peak for carbon at 26° is very strong, which is due to the high quantity of CDs based materials (~95%). The results are accordingly to that of the graphite, showing highly disordered carbon structures [47].

The FT-IR spectra of pristine NHPI and CDs/GQDs @α-Fe2O3 composites are given in Fig. 2 . It is apparent that pristine NHPI records characteristic adsorption bands assigned and discussed in detail in our previous work [48]. The degradation of pristine NHPI in graphite structure (CDs/GQDs) is associated with the disappearance and shifting the position of characteristic adsorption bands of NHPI in FT-IR spectra. The change in position and intensity of stretching vibrations observed in CDs/GQDs @α-Fe2O3 heterostructure (e.g. shortening and widening) suggests formation of graphite structure (CDs/GQDs) functionalized with reactive groups at the surface. Moreover, the ratio of intensities of stretching vibrations observed in CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 composites offers qualitative indications on the magnitude of NHPI modifications during the synthesis. These observations have the implication of a considerable structural damage induced by the self-ignition process on the NHPI. The FT-IR spectra of NHPI-derived CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 exhibited a very broad absorption band between 3700 and 3200 cm−1 assigned to OH stretching vibrations, while the stretching vibrations of aliphatic CH were observed at 2923 cm−1 and 2843 cm−1 respectively. The vibration band at 1633 cm−1 corresponds to CO stretching vibrations, and the band at 1385 cm−1 can be attributed to CO and CC vibrations. Deformation bands of CN and N-O-H are in the region of 1186 cm−1 and the bands in the region of 800–400 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of FeO. The results of FT-IR analysis indicate that CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 composite contain graphite-like structures and abundant oxygen-containing groups mainly carboxyl and hydroxyl groups.

Further, the CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 structure was analyzed by the HRTEM microscopy. The TEM images and size distribution of the CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 composite are displayed in Fig. 3 . The particles are shown to be scattered in regular manner with nearly spherical shape. The inset HRTEM images demonstrate the crystallinity of the CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 heterostructure. Fig. 3 shows a typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 composite. The low magnification TEM image (Fig. 3a) shows individual CDs/GQDs sheets decorated with nanoparticles of 20–80 nm in diameter. The core structure is crystalline and the lattice fringes with an interlayer distance of 0.201 nm correspond to the spacing between (110) planes (FFT image) of α-Fe2O3 crystals, in accordance with XRD data. The carbon-based particles show nearly spherical morphology with uniform size distribution, which can be fitted by a Gaussian curve (Fig. 3c). The size of the carbon-based particles from composite is not very uniform and it is distributed in the 1–5 nm range, whereas the average size is 2.3 nm with a full-width-at-half-maximum (FWHM) of 2 nm as determined by Gaussian fitting analysis.

The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 are shown in Fig. 3b and d. The CDs exhibit a crystalline structure, as revealed by the presence of the lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, which agrees with (100) lattice fringes of graphite. From the line profile analyses of the CDs' diffraction fringes, as shown in Fig. 3d top-left, it can be observed that the distances between the lattice fringes are 0.32 nm. This basal plane distance is slightly smaller than that of bulk graphite (0.335 nm) and is caused most probably by the presence of RFG onto surface as well into the carbon structural matrix. As shown in Fig. 3d, the ultrafine and uniform CDs with sizes of approximately 2 nm were homogeneously anchored on a GQDs sheet, with lattice spacing of 0.212 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of crystalline graphite and (100) spacing of single carbon dots. Interestingly, the unchanged lattice distance of 0.212 nm [49] and a hexagonal pattern from fast Fourier transform (FFT) image indicate that the successfully formation of embedded GQD during the novel synthesis method. The HRTEM results clearly confirmed that the CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 heterostructure was successfully synthesized using a facile novel SI-FC method.

In Fig. 4 , the emission spectra recorded within 320–420 nm excitation range (10 nm steps) are presented. The prepared CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 composite present the characteristic excitation dependent emission [50,51]. As it could be noted, the emission peaks shift from 419 to 510 nm within the 320–420 nm excitation range. The maximum emission intensity is achieved at 390 nm excitation, the emission peak being located at 503 nm in the blue-green region of the visible spectrum.

The field dependence of the magnetization of the carbon is presented in Fig. 5 . The hysteresis loop shows a value of saturation magnetization of 18 emu/g at an applied field of 20 kOe and temperature of 300 K. The field coercivity, Hc, of 0.085 kOe and remanent magnetization, Mr. of 2 emu/g indicate the absence of the superparamagnetic behavior (Fig. 5 - inset a). The magnetization value of the embedded hematite nanoparticles was calculated after adjusting for hematite content in the sample (5%) considering the phase quantification determined from XRD analysis. According to the saturation magnetization values, hematite (α-Fe2O3) is conventionally supposed to be a non-magnetic material. However, several reports have recently shown that hematite nanostructures can display remarkable magnetic properties, at least in terms of saturation magnetization [52]. Such improved magnetic properties can be induced generally at temperatures between 350 and 400 °C, the reaction temperature being a key parameter [53]. In our case, even if the saturation magnetization is inferior to that recently found for hematite nanoparticles obtained at high temperatures [47,52], it highly exceeds the values of conventionally-prepared hematite whose saturation magnetization is typically less than 1 emu/g [52]. The increased magnetization can be related to the particle clustering and contribution of uncompensated surface spins, leading to ferromagnetic interactions [52,54]. Uncompensated surface spins surrounding the core of each Fe2O3 tend to interact ferromagnetically, exhibiting high magnetization values when the spins are aligned by an external magnetic field [54]. When the nanoparticles forms compacted clusters, the uncompensated spins of each Fe2O3 nanoparticle interact ferromagnetically also with their neighbours spins, leading to increased magnetization. In other words, the magnetization of an individual Fe2O3 nanoparticle in the cluster will be higher than the magnetization of the same nanoparticle measured separated from the cluster.

Sonication and sterilization can impact the electrical charge, size and, consecutively, colloidal stability of the nanoparticles [55,56]. In this study, the variations of particle size, zeta potential and polydispersity index of the carbon particles were assessed before and after sonication and sterilization. The zeta potential decreased from −18.8 to −20, hydrodynamic diameter decreased from 782 nm 710 nm, while polydispersity index decreased from 0.912 to 0.521 (57% variation). Therefore, following sterilization and ultra-sonication, the colloidal stability was slightly improved especially due to the decrease of agglomeration and slight increase of the absolute value of zeta potential which finally led to an improved ionic binding of the positively charged MIT.

The cumulative release of MIT from carbon particles dispersed in the releasing media (CCM) was assessed for about 8 h at pH 7.4 and 5.2, respectively (Fig. 6 ). For measurements at pH 7.4, the initial drug content of the carbon particles was 3.2% (w/w) and loading efficiency of 98.18%, while for measurements at pH 5.2, the drug content was 3.1% (w/w) and loading efficiency of 97.5%.

An important burst release was noted during the first hour, leading to MIT discharge of 6.7%. The initial burst release might be the result of a significant displacement of the slightly ionically bounded MIT by ionic species and serum albumins within the cell culture medium. After the initial burst release, during the remaining 7 h, the MIT discharge process significantly slowed down, adding only 1.2% to the initial release of MIT. The decrease of the release rate is due to the stronger binding of the remained MIT to the carbon particles mainly through ionic and π–π interactions. The latter interaction is specific for carbon particles when coupling with drugs containing benzene rings such as doxorubicin [57]. However, it is worth to add that even if the drug release profile would be largely kept in the case of further-performed in vitro tests, the concentration of the released MIT over the cells would be different from the bulk culture medium. Basically, following the settling of the carbon particles on the cells, part of the drug would be sequestrated within the cell-particle interface until the cell uptake. Therefore, we suppose that in the interface zone the drug concentration is much higher than that found in the bulk cell medium. Moreover, the drug could also be sequestrated between the layers of settled carbon particles, leading consequently to the decrease of the anti-tumor effect, as demonstrated for the progressive increase of the MIT-coated carbon doses which have changed only slightly the anti-proliferative effect compared to the lowest doses of MIT-coated CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 (see below in Section 3.4).

In this study, the influence of CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 particles on cell viability was quantified through the MTT assay as expression of the mitochondrial dehydrogenase activity. At increasing concentrations of CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 particles, ranging from 0.01 mg/mL to 0.32 mg/mL, after 24 h the cell viability varied between 82% and 92% for fibroblasts incubated with CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 particles coated or uncoated by MIT (Fig. 7a, b). By contrary, the osteosarcoma cells showed a marked viability decrease, especially for composite coated with MIT, down to 46% (Fig. 7b). It was however interesting to observe that even the MIT-free CDs based particles managed to highly affect tumor cells, with a maximum damaging effect at 0.04 mg/mL, when 44% of the osteosarcoma cells were killed (Fig. 7a). However, for higher concentrations, the effect was less intense probably due to a reduced upload of the CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 particles by the cells since at higher concentration the particles were observed to slightly cluster. Such clusters probably need more than 24 h to be uploaded by cells to induce apoptosis. For MIT-coated composite, the killing effect was more pronounced, leading to the death of 64% of osteosarcoma cells for the highest concentration of the particles i.e., 0.32 mg/mL. By comparison, only 15% of fibroblasts were irreversibly affected by MIT-coated CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 particles (Fig. 7b). The anti-tumor effect of the NHPI-derived carbon particles are most probably the result of the anti-proliferative effects of the N-OH functional group of the NHPI [43], which partially remained stable after NHPI combustion at a temperature of the sand bath of 250 °C. FTIR characterization of both NHPI and NHPI-derived carbon particles confirmed this supposition (Fig. 2). On the other side, since the CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 particles contains hydrophobic parts resulted from the glycine-NHPI combustion, their lipophilic properties increase their uptake by the cells as previously reported for NHPI modified with lipophilic radicals [43]. Basically, the combustion led to NHPI-derivatives, some of which having anti-tumor effect as underlined by Melone et al. [43].

In order to link the anti-tumor effect of the NHPI-derived carbon particles with the NHPI's effect, a cytotoxicity evaluation was separately conducted. The tests have confirmed the results obtained by Melone et al. [43] regarding the anti-tumor effect of the NHPI on osteosarcoma cells only at high doses. In their excellent analysis, Melone et al. reported that unmodified NHPI do not display cell anti-proliferative activity at usual concentrations. Further, modified NHPI with different lipophilic groups showed an increased potential to induce intracellular formation of reactive oxygen species, causing cell death through depletion of glutathione (GSH) and increased oxidative stress [43]. In our experiments, we obtained identical decreases of viability as Melone but at lower concentrations of NHPI simply because we have used a lower density of osteosarcoma cells, i.e. 104 per well vs. 1.5 × 104 in Melone's experiments. At the lowest concentration, i.e. 0.01 mg/mL, the NHPI has no cytotoxic effect on osteosarcoma cells (Fig. 7c). However, for the same concentration, in the presence of CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 particles or MIT-coated particles, the tumor cells died in a proportion of 37% (vs. 18% normal cells) or 47% (vs. 16% normal cells), respectively. This is a clear indication that carbon particles themselves are selectively toxic, with higher impact on the osteosarcoma cells, due to their increased lipophilicity and remained residual N-OH active groups. The MIT boosted the anti-tumor effect by about 10%.

The intrinsic antitumor effect of the synthesized CDs/GQDs@α-Fe2O3 particles can be linked to the increase of the reactive oxygen species, followed by depletion of intracellular glutathione which leads to cell apoptosis via caspase activation, as Melone et al. underlined for chemically-modified lipophilic NHPI [43]. Hence, the obtained results underscore the high potential of the NHPI-derived CDs, produced by a fast-cooling sol-gel self-ignited reaction, to selectively affect a tumor cell line (i.e. osteosarcoma cells) resilient to an unmodified molecule with antitumor properties (i.e., NHPI) administrated at standard doses. Moreover, the newly developed synthesis method for CDs allows a good control of particle size through ultra-sonication and, possibly, ball-milling, and surface functionalization by incorporation of antitumor drugs to be directed to specific tumors. The composite also allows for modulation of the drug release through the inherent properties of the magnetic iron oxide, linking the CDs-based material with imaging (e.g. MRI) and cancer therapy. Besides, due to their relatively low toxicity and appropriate aqueous dispersibility, the as-prepared CDs/GQDs@Fe2O3 heterostructure suggest a fine potential in biosensing, cellular imaging and drug delivery applications.

In this work, a new antitumor nanocomposite was synthesized, characterized and evaluated for possible applications in cancer therapy. The lipophilic, fluorescent, and magnetic carbon dots-graphene composite was prepared by a newly flash-cooling assisted sol-gel method through the combustion of glycine and NHPI - a recently investigated cost-effective anti-tumor molecule. The obtained heterostructure showed a good potential for inducing by itself the death of tumor cells, whereas further functionalization with an antitumor drug (i.e., mitoxantrone) boosted the killing effect up to 47% after only 24 h of treatment. The NHPI-derived CDs nanocomposite showed to be active against osteosarcoma cells at concentrations 16 times lower than those used for pristine NHPI to obtain a similar killing effect. Moreover, the ratio modifies to about 1:30 if the NHPI's effect is compared to that induced by the MIT-functionalized heterostructure. The method has an almost 100% yield and is very flexible, being prone to modifications in terms of (a) product final composition through simultaneous combustion of different cocktails of antitumor drugs, (b) improved magnetization by starting with already-prepared high-magnetization magnetic nanoparticles, (c) size of the nanocomposite through optimized ball-milling process, etc. However, in our opinion the cornerstone of this method resides in its potential to afford a series of antitumor drugs an increased lipophilicity after separate or in-cocktail combustion in order to highly improve the drug availability to tumor cells. This is highly appropriate for antitumor drugs with reduced lipophilicity.

A. Borhan, D.D. Herea, and D. Gherca conceived, designed, performed the synthesis experiments and co-wrote the paper, C. Stavila, A. Minuti C. Danceanu, L. Labusca performed the cell-related experiments and interpreted the results, G. Stoian co-wrote the paper, G. Ababei performed HR-TEM measurements and processed the images, C. Stan performed and interpreted the fluorescence measurements, N. Lupu and H. Chiriac performed data analysis and manuscript revision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the UEFISCDI grants, Project no. 37 PCCDI/2018 – Teradot.
